title: History of the Philippines

stack.label: Captaincy General of the Philippines (Spanish East Indies)
Sultanate of Sulu (resists Spain — partial independence)
Insular Government of the Philippine Islands
Fourth Republic — Marcos martial law era

footnotes: Austronesian settlement (c. 3000 BCE): Austronesian-speaking peoples from Taiwan migrated southward through the Philippines, spreading throughout Maritime Southeast Asia and the Pacific; the Philippine archipelago served as a key waypoint in this expansion; indigenous Negrito peoples (Aeta, Agta) predate the Austronesian migrations

Pre-colonial polities: the Philippines had no single unified state before Spanish colonization; instead hundreds of barangays (kinship-based communities) and several larger polities coexisted; Tondo was the most prominent Luzon polity, with ties to Majapahit and later Brunei; the Rajahnate of Butuan is documented in Chinese Song dynasty records as early as 1001 CE

Islamization of Mindanao and Sulu: Islam arrived in the Sulu archipelago via Muslim traders from Borneo and Malacca in the late 14th–early 15th century; the Sultanate of Sulu (c. 1405) and Sultanate of Maguindanao (c. 1500) were established before Spanish arrival; both sultanates successfully resisted full Spanish conquest, maintaining de facto autonomy throughout the colonial period

Spanish colonial period (1565–1898): Spain claimed the archipelago through Legazpi's expedition in 1565, naming it after Philip II; Manila was founded in 1571 on the site of the former Tondo polity; the Philippines was administered as part of the Viceroyalty of New Spain (Mexico) until 1821, then directly from Madrid; the Manila Galleon trade linked Acapulco to Manila for 250 years

Philippine Revolution and First Republic (1896–1901): the Katipunan secret society launched the revolution against Spain in 1896; Emilio Aguinaldo proclaimed independence on 12 June 1898 (now Independence Day); the Malolos Constitution established the First Philippine Republic in 1899, the first constitutional republic in Asia; the Philippine–American War (1899–1902) ended the republic

American colonial period (1898–1946): the US acquired the Philippines from Spain for $20 million via the Treaty of Paris (1898); the Insular Government introduced public education (English-medium), infrastructure, and Philippine autonomy under the Jones Act (1916); the Tydings–McDuffie Act (1934) created the Commonwealth as a transition to independence

Japanese Occupation (1942–1945): Japan invaded in December 1941 and established the Second Philippine Republic under José Laurel in 1943 as a nominally independent puppet state; President Quezon led the Commonwealth government-in-exile from Washington; the Battle of Manila (1945) caused catastrophic civilian casualties and destroyed the historic walled city of Intramuros

Marcos era and EDSA Revolution: Ferdinand Marcos declared martial law in September 1972, effectively ending the Third Republic; his authoritarian Fourth Republic lasted until the People Power Revolution (EDSA Revolution) of February 1986, which peacefully ousted Marcos and restored democracy under Corazon Aquino; the Fifth Republic has operated continuously since then
